Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study later, and then later never came. Sometimes I'd sit down with my headphones thinking I'd listen to lecture recordings, but I'd end up just scrolling through my phone instead. The weeks kind of blurred together and before I knew it, assignments were due and I hadn't done anything.
Q2: My pattern was basically chaos. I'd only really do anything when a deadline was literally the next day, and even then sometimes I just couldn't bring myself to care enough. There was no regular schedule—it depended entirely on how anxious I was feeling about failing, which ironically made me avoid things more.
Q3: Usually panic. Like, I'd realize I hadn't checked in for weeks and suddenly worry I'd missed something huge. Sometimes my peer support group would message me asking if I'd seen something on the LMS, and that would guilt me into logging in. It was never because I actually wanted to be there.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became routine for me. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by all the things I'd missed, and then log out feeling worse than before. The few times I tried to make it a habit, something would come up or I'd just lose interest completely.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful when I actually watched them, because I learn better by listening. I could put them on and absorb some information without having to read walls of text. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing other students confused made me feel less alone in not understanding things.
Q6: I ignored most of the reading materials and the practice quizzes. Reading just doesn't work for me—the words blur together and I lose focus immediately. I kept telling myself I'd get to them eventually, but eventually never happened. They felt like busywork that I couldn't bring myself to care about.
Q7: Honestly? If it felt quick and easy, I'd do it. If something looked like it would take hours of concentration, my brain just shut down. The activities that felt worth it were ones where I could see immediate progress or where other students were also participating so I didn't feel so isolated.
Q8: Required activities stressed me out more, but I still didn't do most of them. Optional ones I completely ignored because my brain was like 'why bother if it doesn't count?' It's messed up logic, I know, but when you're already drowning, optional stuff feels like asking someone to swim extra laps.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture audio and listen to it while doing other things, like walking or trying to fall asleep. I also had a friend who'd share their notes with me, so I'd study from those instead of going through the LMS materials. It felt less overwhelming that way.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours talking with classmates from my peer support group about course concepts, trying to understand things through conversation. None of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times but the LMS only knew I accessed them once.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture recording once and then replay it several times offline over the following days. The LMS probably thought I watched it for like twenty minutes, but I actually listened to some of them three or four times while doing other stuff.
Q12: It got really hard around midterms when everything piled up at once. I have pretty bad anxiety, and when I saw how behind I was, I just froze. I couldn't make myself do anything—it was like being paralyzed. The sadness would hit and I'd just stay in bed instead of facing it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other things in life, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Seeing all those incomplete tasks and missed deadlines just made my anxiety spike. Sometimes I'd open it, see the mountain of work, and immediately close it to protect myself.
Q14: Yeah, lots of times. I'd have the LMS open and be clicking through things, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed a single word. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important, but the anxiety made me avoid them. It's like my brain convinced me that if I didn't look at it, it wasn't real. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd say the same thing until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me to do anything, but by the time I reacted, it was usually too late to do quality work. I'd panic-submit something half-done or just miss it entirely. The pressure didn't motivate me—it paralyzed me until the last possible second.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes helped me understand what I was doing wrong. But honestly, by the time feedback came, I'd already mentally checked out of that assignment. It felt like getting notes on a ship that had already sunk.
Q18: The course structure felt really rigid and unforgiving. Once you fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up. There was so much content spread across so many weeks that missing a few classes meant you were lost. It didn't accommodate people who struggle to stay consistent.
Q19: Having seventeen online courses made everything harder—it was just too much to track. The LMS interface itself was overwhelming with all the notifications and deadlines. If things had been simpler or more flexible, maybe I could have kept up better.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying, you know? They're logging in, clicking around, genuinely attempting to engage, but they can't translate that into finished work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, or they get stuck and don't know how to move forward. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. Some people are just efficient—they grab what they need and study offline. Or they're smart enough to not need all the extra stuff. I wish I could be like that, but for me, low activity meant I was genuinely struggling, not secretly succeeding.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the studying that happens away from the computer, all the conversations with classmates, all the times you're staring at the ceiling trying to process information.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through motions to pass. It means being mentally present and curious. I don't think I was truly engaged this semester—I was just surviving, barely.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling emotionally. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before trying to start an assignment, or the sadness that kept me in bed for days. It can't show that I actually cared about doing well but my brain wouldn't cooperate. The human stuff is invisible.
